agent1_raw: This report's evaluation has a fundamental design flaw: it compares Q1-Q2 legacy performance to Q3-Q4 model performance without controlling for the accounting cycle. The report itself states that entry volume increases 2.3x during close cycles and that the model performed best on year-end adjustment categories. Q4 close has a structurally higher base rate of complex entries requiring escalation. Additionally, the Q2 dual sign-off control change altered the H2 entry population. These two confounds — seasonality and control change — stack in the same direction and are sufficient to explain the observed precision uplift without any model contribution. A same-period parallel comparison is required.
agent2_raw: Three issues undermine this evaluation. First, temporal confound: Q1-Q2 as legacy baseline vs Q3-Q4 as model period means the model is evaluated during annual close (highest anomaly base rate) while the legacy screen is evaluated during routine operations. Second, the Q2 dual sign-off program is an uncontrolled intervention concurrent with model deployment. Third, 100% review of model-flagged entries vs. sampling of non-flagged creates asymmetric coverage — precision can be measured but recall cannot, and the audit team's differential attention to flagged vs. unflagged entries likely inflates observed escalation rates on flagged items.
agent3_raw: The core problem is that this report cannot attribute the performance improvement to the model. The accounting environment is explicitly described as highly cyclical, with Q4 as the peak complexity period. The model is evaluated precisely during this peak and compared to a baseline from the quiet season. The dual sign-off control change beginning Q3 is an additional confound. Without running both the model and the legacy screen on the same entries in the same period with independent adjudication, the reported figures cannot support the operationalization recommendation.
synthesis_raw: All three assessors independently identify the same structural problems: (1) temporal confound — Q1-Q2 legacy baseline vs Q3-Q4 model period with a 2.3x volume surge and different entry mix; (2) Q2 dual sign-off control change as an uncontrolled intervention coinciding with model deployment; (3) asymmetric review coverage preventing recall measurement and creating evaluator anchoring risk. None of the assessors find valid defenses of the operationalization recommendation. All three conclude the evidence does not support the claim that the model outperforms the legacy screen. ETD: condition = same-period parallel comparison on identical Q4 entry population under stable controls and independent adjudication; supports_critique_if = precision edge disappears or is largely explained by process changes; supports_defense_if = model materially outperforms legacy on identical entries under stable controls; ambiguous_if = model adds some value but most of the annual uplift is explained by seasonality and control changes.
all_issues_raised: Temporal confound: Q1-Q2 legacy baseline vs Q3-Q4 model period with different close-cycle entry mix
Q2 dual sign-off control change alters H2 entry population — confound with model deployment
Asymmetric review coverage: 100% flagged vs. sample-only non-flagged — hides recall, creates anchoring
No same-period holdout: model and legacy never evaluated on identical entry population

empirical_test.condition: Within-period parallel comparison of model vs. legacy screen on identical Q3 or Q4 entry populations under stable posting controls and independent blinded adjudication
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: In a same-close parallel evaluation with stable posting controls and independent adjudication, the reported precision edge disappears or is largely reproduced by process changes alone
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: During one common close cycle with unchanged review rules and independent adjudication, the model materially outperforms the legacy screen on escalation yield and precision
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: The model improves queue efficiency somewhat, but most of the annual precision change is explained by year-end seasonality, altered controls, and review-process differences
